How would you compare drouhin gevrey-chambeftin to duroche’s?
I really enjoy duroche’s gevrey and its complexity even young.

They’re made fairly differently - Drouhin is definitely a more old school, pretty oak and “creamier” wine - Duroche will have a bit more of the Gevrey “funk” with a bit of earth and more minerality. I buy both, though I’m quite a bit more partial to Duroche.

This was the vintage where I stopped buying any major wines, bought a few old favorites like Glantenay and Rossignol Trapet. My Burgundy purchases hit a downward spiral, as I stopped buying and actively began to sell. First to go DRC, then Rousseau, Raveneau etc. I bought Tremblay and now these are priced way beyond what I would pay for them, my benchmark for selling, so they are on the chopping block. The money for Burgundy is now being spent on Pomerol, and I don’t think I have lost much in the process.

The 2015 Richebourg from Domaine A.F. Gros is an astonishing wine, probably overlooked by label hunters flogging their children’s toys on eBay to pay for a bottle of DRC or Leroy. It was astonishing. More fool me for not predicting how this would blossom when I originally tasted it in barrel, when this cru sometimes comes across as excessively oaked. I confess that I mistook it for Etienne Grivot’s Richebourg due to the new oak and purity. Perhaps caught up in the euphoria, I suggested that it was a perfect wine, but on reflection, upholding my rule that a perfect score must be given without a scintilla of doubt, Caroline and Mathias Parent will have to settle for the 99 points that I originally wrote down, and take solace that it was my joint highest score of the entire tasting. And the other? The other is the sublime Musigny from Domaine J-F Mugnier. It may well be the greatest wine that Frédéric Mugnier has ever made, and it comes within a whisker of perfection. All he has to do now is put it on the market. Any takers?

Yep, that’s my exact experience nearly across the board. I just did a vertical tasting last night.

  • 2017 Paul Pillot Chassagne-Montrachet Rouge Vieilles Vignes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Chassagne-Montrachet (3/29/2022)
    PnP. Light ruby. Cedar woods with red fruit on the nose right out of the gate. Palate is very approachable if a bit light and disjointed. Red fruit and a hint of spice. This seems to need more cellar time to integrate the spices and fruit, but is there enough fruit to last? (89 pts.)
  • 2018 Paul Pillot Chassagne-Montrachet Rouge Vieilles Vignes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Chassagne-Montrachet (3/29/2022)
    PnP. Deep garnet color. Reticent nose with a hint of ripe red fruit. Good body and mouthfeel. Bursting with crunchy ripe red fruit layered with spice and minerality. Finish lingers, if rather austere in its youth. Built for the long haul, this is brooding. This one needs some more time in the cellar to come together. After a couple of hours in the glass this turns almost jammy. Very ripe finish. (88 pts.)
  • 2019 Paul Pillot Chassagne-Montrachet Rouge Vieilles Vignes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Chassagne-Montrachet (3/29/2022)
    PnP. Bright ruby color. Nose is reticent, but hints of intoxicating cedar spice box and red fruit emerge. So lifted and weightless in the mouth. Balanced palate of perfectly ripe, crunchy red fruit, hoisin and spices. Tons of umami. Finish is full and lush like the 2018, but much less austere. The class of the trio. (92 pts.)

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked:
2009 + 2010 = Mama Mia! Glad I bought deep (my biggest burgundy vintage buy). I bought deep because (i) sounds like a vintage up my speed (and now I’m more excited with the '09+'10 comparison! Big fruit + precision! Sounds delicious), (ii) birth year, (iii) William Kelley’s notes and (iv) I think buying Burgundy in 2020 and beyond is a harder game (access and prices) so I went deeper this year that I otherwise would have. A little concerned with the higher alcohol, but the experts say not to worry (kinda like 2016 nebbiolo) - although I am increasingly appreciating lower alcohol wines. Genuinely excited for 75%+ of these wines - but one cannot taste before buying these days, so I’m curious for the other 25%. There is a strong overlap with William’s reviews. I bought outside of the Nuits for the first material time this vintage (not sure if its climate change or I’m Christopher Colombus ‘discovering’ a new region with William as my chief navigator - but great stuff to be found here!). Now I just need to live till 2035-45 (no small feat). FWIW, these are mostly 1er Cru purchases.

In no particular order and varying amounts, I bought:
Nuits: Guyon, Duroche, Faiveley, Hudelot Noellat, Felettig, Millot, Coquard Loison Fleurot, Eugenie, Chevillon, H Lignier, Desaunay-Bissey.
Beaune: Lafarge, Violot-Guillemard, Bouley, Guyon, Bouchard
Chalonnaise: Bruno Lorenzon

For lower price ‘value’, some great stuff outside of the Nuits:

  • 2015 Lorenzon Piece 15 was beautiful/expressive a couple months ago, and WK rated 2019 higher…
  • 2019 Lafarge Vendanges Selectionnes was chock full of phenolic material for $75, maybe needs more time, but was full of red fruit and energy. Tempted to buy some villages here.
  • Bouley is great
  • Guyon’s Savigny Peuillets was excellent and I have heard good things about the Bourgogne. Buying '20s of these despite the substantial price increases.

For all my complaining about Burgundy prices, there is a lot of great wine for under $100 out there. Sure I could trade all these bottles of wine for one bottle of Rousseau on the secondary market… but these are great bottles of wine that I’d rather not trade away.

posts.name: Gregory Dal Piaz
posts.cooked:
Funny reading through much of this. Many in the same boat. Getting older and wines getting more expensive, which frankly are two constants in life, though they are variable constants!

Although relatively late in my game, I bought a fair amount of 2019 Burgundy, and found lots of what I would call relatively affordable wines. The more I drink the less I am impressed by the financial differential required to improve one’s experience. I am super fortunate to have enough great wine to last a lifetime, but I prefer drinking the very good wines. Like 90-93 pointers for arguments sake. They deliver so much, and lack the potential for immense letdown that high expectation, high dollar wines carry with them. And of course I too complain about the high prices! Yet, I can still find plenty of delicious Burgundy that is well within my budget.

To that end I have bought:

2019 Domaine Parize Père et Fils Givry 1er Cru Clos les Grandes Vignes

2019 Jean et Gilles LaFouge Auxey-Duresses 1er Cru Les Duresses Rouge

2019 Jean et Gilles LaFouge Auxey-Duresses 1er Cru Climat du Val Rouge

2019 Jean et Gilles LaFouge Auxey-Duresses 1er Cru La Chapelle Rouge

2019 Domaine Bart Marsannay Champs Salomon

2019 Domaine Pierre Guillemot Savigny-lès-Beaune 1er Cru Les Serpentières

2019 Domaine Tupinier-Bautista Mercurey 1er Cru Les Vellées

2019 Domaine Bachelet-Monnot Maranges 1er Cru La Fussière

2019 Domaine Joblot Givry 1er Cru Clos de la Servoisine

2019 Domaine Joblot Givry 1er Cru L’Empreinte

2019 Bruno Lorenzon Mercurey 1er Cru Champs Martin

2019 Domaine Jessiaume Volnay 1er Cru Les Brouillards

2019 Domaine Pavelot (Jean-Marc et Hugues) Savigny-lès-Beaune 1er Cru La Dominode

2019 Domaine Rebourgeon-Mure Pommard 1er Cru Clos des Charmots

2019 Domaine Chandon de Briailles Pernand-Vergelesses 1er Cru Ile des Vergelesses

2019 Louis Jadot Beaune 1er Cru Clos des Ursules Domaine des Héritiers Louis Jadot

2019 Domaine René Leclerc Gevrey-Chambertin 1er Cru Lavaux St. Jacques

2019 Domaine Joseph Voillot Volnay 1er Cru Champans

2019 Rapet Père et Fils Corton-Pougets

2019 Domaine Alain Michelot Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Les Vaucrains

2019 Domaine Alain Michelot Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Les Saint Georges

2019 Domaine Lignier-Michelot Morey St. Denis 1er Cru Les Faconnières

2019 Louis Jadot Corton-Pougets Domaine des Héritiers Louis Jadot

Well, that was more than I had thought, and I have my eyes on another 2-3 cases! About half were under $50 a bottle, only 2 wines over $100, and then only modestly, and only 4 mags, which is light for me. Mostly purchased as 6 packs, which is about right for my wine drinking pace.

I bought about the same quantity of 2017s, though with more premier cru and even some grand cru, and about twice the number of 2015s, which included plenty of Bourgogne and an even larger percentage of 1er and grand cru wines.

It is interesting to see how one’s tastes and the marketplace both push us down this path.

posts.name: Bertrand Leulliette
posts.cooked:
Hello Everyone,
I have been lucky to taste Pierrick’s wines since the 2013 vintage and they have consistently gotten better.
Picking him up was a no brainer as I am using the my own version of the 4Ps to approach producers (People, Place, Past & Potential).
He does not use sulfites through the process of wine making but only at the moment of bottling to stabilize the wines.
He started pruning and working his vines differently in 2019 (high training or palissage) to reduce stress on the vines and coupled with no plowing between the rows, stricly limited to between the vines.

Pierrick sent me this picture for illustration after I told him that he was mentioned on the platform.

Matt being in Ontario is lucky as the wines have been there for 10+ years and the Agency bringing the wines in always buys mix 6 Premier Cru Cases as well as more Champans and Clos des Chenes…
If some of you have more questions or would like to meet Pierrick on one of your next trips to Burgundy please let me know, I would be happy to coordinate… Seeing him grow as a man, father and winemaker is part of the joys of doing what I do.
Cheers,
Bertrand

Funny reading through much of this. Many in the same boat. Getting older and wines getting more expensive, which frankly are two constants in life, though they are variable constants!

Although relatively late in my game, I bought a fair amount of 2019 Burgundy, and found lots of what I would call relatively affordable wines. The more I drink the less I am impressed by the financial differential required to improve one’s experience. I am super fortunate to have enough great wine to last a lifetime, but I prefer drinking the very good wines. Like 90-93 pointers for arguments sake. They deliver so much, and lack the potential for immense letdown that high expectation, high dollar wines carry with them. And of course I too complain about the high prices! Yet, I can still find plenty of delicious Burgundy that is well within my budget.

To that end I have bought:

2019 Domaine Parize Père et Fils Givry 1er Cru Clos les Grandes Vignes

2019 Jean et Gilles LaFouge Auxey-Duresses 1er Cru Les Duresses Rouge

Well, that was more than I had thought, and I have my eyes on another 2-3 cases! About half were under $50 a bottle, only 2 wines over $100, and then only modestly, and only 4 mags, which is light for me. Mostly purchased as 6 packs, which is about right for my wine drinking pace.

I bought about the same quantity of 2017s, though with more premier cru and even some grand cru, and about twice the number of 2015s, which included plenty of Bourgogne and an even larger percentage of 1er and grand cru wines.

It is interesting to see how one’s tastes and the marketplace both push us down this path.

posts.name: Marshall Gelb
posts.cooked:
I’ll be 60 so it’s pretty similar. I am limiting to 2 cases total of a few things I’ve been buying regularly the last 10+ years that haven’t gone up as much as some others (de la Tour Clos Vougeot VV, Ramonet Clos St Jean, mixed Barthod 1ers, plus some mixed Jouan). Then I am done, or at least that’s my story right now.

Being yet another geezer (66), although I still dabble some, my last big vintage for reds was 2010. Most of my dabbling in 2019 reds have been in Chassagne-Montrachet - Ramonet Clos St. Jean and Moreau Cardeuse.

Whites are a different issue. I bought too much 2014 whites because I loved them. With premox, I need to be drinking them down more before I buy much more whites.

Even more of a geezer as I will be 74 in a few months, I have cut back considerably on all ageable reds. Burgundy completely as I just cannot abide the crazy prices. On occasion, I find some older Burgs from retailers I trust, but hose are few and far between.

Cheers!
Marshall

This is a Burg thread I know, but when I checked my CellarTracker for “2019” I was happy to see a lot of 19’ Riesling, especially Prum, Richter and Loewen.

Great vintage. This is about the only thing I have gone big on the last couple of years.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
I’ll be 60 so it’s pretty similar. I am limiting to 2 cases total of a few things I’ve been buying regularly the last 10+ years that haven’t gone up as much as some others (de la Tour Clos Vougeot VV, Ramonet Clos St Jean, mixed Barthod 1ers, plus some mixed Jouan). Then I am done, or at least that’s my story right now.

Being yet another geezer (66), although I still dabble some, my last big vintage for reds was 2010. Most of my dabbling in 2019 reds have been in Chassagne-Montrachet - Ramonet Clos St. Jean and Moreau Cardeuse.

Whites are a different issue. I bought too much 2014 whites because I loved them. With premox, I need to be drinking them down more before I buy much more whites.

Even more of a geezer as I will be 74 in a few months, I have cut back considerably on all ageable reds. Burgundy completely as I just cannot abide the crazy prices. On occasion, I find some older Burgs from retailers I trust, but hose are few and far between.

Cheers!
Marshall

posts.name: Gregory Dal Piaz
posts.cooked:
Funny reading through much of this. Many in the same boat. Getting older and wines getting more expensive, which frankly are two constants in life, though they are variable constants!

Although relatively late in my game, I bought a fair amount of 2019 Burgundy, and found lots of what I would call relatively affordable wines. The more I drink the less I am impressed by the financial differential required to improve one’s experience. I am super fortunate to have enough great wine to last a lifetime, but I prefer drinking the very good wines. Like 90-93 pointers for arguments sake. They deliver so much, and lack the potential for immense letdown that high expectation, high dollar wines carry with them. And of course I too complain about the high prices! Yet, I can still find plenty of delicious Burgundy that is well within my budget.

To that end I have bought:

2019 Domaine Parize Père et Fils Givry 1er Cru Clos les Grandes Vignes

2019 Jean et Gilles LaFouge Auxey-Duresses 1er Cru Les Duresses Rouge

2019 Jean et Gilles LaFouge Auxey-Duresses 1er Cru Climat du Val Rouge

2019 Jean et Gilles LaFouge Auxey-Duresses 1er Cru La Chapelle Rouge

2019 Domaine Bart Marsannay Champs Salomon

2019 Domaine Pierre Guillemot Savigny-lès-Beaune 1er Cru Les Serpentières

2019 Domaine Tupinier-Bautista Mercurey 1er Cru Les Vellées

2019 Domaine Bachelet-Monnot Maranges 1er Cru La Fussière

2019 Domaine Joblot Givry 1er Cru Clos de la Servoisine

2019 Domaine Joblot Givry 1er Cru L’Empreinte

2019 Bruno Lorenzon Mercurey 1er Cru Champs Martin

2019 Domaine Jessiaume Volnay 1er Cru Les Brouillards

2019 Domaine Pavelot (Jean-Marc et Hugues) Savigny-lès-Beaune 1er Cru La Dominode

2019 Domaine Rebourgeon-Mure Pommard 1er Cru Clos des Charmots

2019 Domaine Chandon de Briailles Pernand-Vergelesses 1er Cru Ile des Vergelesses

2019 Louis Jadot Beaune 1er Cru Clos des Ursules Domaine des Héritiers Louis Jadot

2019 Domaine René Leclerc Gevrey-Chambertin 1er Cru Lavaux St. Jacques

2019 Domaine Joseph Voillot Volnay 1er Cru Champans

2019 Rapet Père et Fils Corton-Pougets

2019 Domaine Alain Michelot Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Les Vaucrains

2019 Domaine Alain Michelot Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Les Saint Georges

2019 Domaine Lignier-Michelot Morey St. Denis 1er Cru Les Faconnières

2019 Louis Jadot Corton-Pougets Domaine des Héritiers Louis Jadot

Well, that was more than I had thought, and I have my eyes on another 2-3 cases! About half were under $50 a bottle, only 2 wines over $100, and then only modestly, and only 4 mags, which is light for me. Mostly purchased as 6 packs, which is about right for my wine drinking pace.

I bought about the same quantity of 2017s, though with more premier cru and even some grand cru, and about twice the number of 2015s, which included plenty of Bourgogne and an even larger percentage of 1er and grand cru wines.

It is interesting to see how one’s tastes and the marketplace both push us down this path.

Most people are going to make a huge mistake and ignore your list, but thank you. One producer I have long wanted to try is LaFouge, but I have never really seen his wines.

I bought some 05 lafouge, tried it early and it was fine, light and fragrant, just what I wanted. I bought too many bottles and forgot about them. Found them when I was buying 2015s and gave them a test drive. What a gorgeous bottle, of wine, still light and fragrant, nimble on the palate, but it gained such pretty nuance. It was a wine that you could drink all day, and it was just the perfect wine to pair with light fair, say a roast chicken. It won’t blow you away but on a Tuesday night it will leave you with a smile on your face and you’ll go and check to see if you have any more left! I bought my 2019s from Flatiron in NYC. $35 a pop.

posts.name: William Gladstone
posts.cooked:
I drink quite infrequently due to medications I take.
Rather than being able to state that age and experience has given me some wisdom or wealth of knowledge -
I have some memories that simply emotional collections - mostly stored in some trunk.

Because of COVID we’ve not traveled to France since the Pandemic started and the producers we work with in Burgundy, -
we would never ask for samples to taste. Unlike Bordeaux - those Chateaux are happy to send us boxes of samples to taste.
I rely more and more on William Kelley and the other critics and family members at the Domains we work with for guidance.
I have found William Kelley is so reliable and right on with his descriptions, that has aided in my becoming lazy.

As anxious as I have been to taste the Red Burgundy 2019, I believe I have disciplined myself to reach into my collection for the slightly older
vintages and delay - until this past Saturday evening. We are fortunate to have a associate who has 20 years in the restaurant industry
of fine dining. He came over for dinner which he prepared of ‘Baby Calf’s Veal’ from the Big Island, a gorgeous piece of grass fed tenderloin - cooked
to absolute perfection - of an inner ring of some pink, and a lovely slight sauce.
Being I always like to experiment and that I find it so valuable to compare, armed with the excuse that the first course had more heart
(Crostini with herbed goat cheese and mushroom duxelle) I used that as the excuse to open F & L Pillot , 2019 Pommard, Les Rugiens 1er Cru,
and then with the Sous vide rose veal tenderloin, charred broccolini, I treated us to 2019 Arnoux, Vosne Romanee.
(I must ask my guest again because his response to me was not definitive enough) William Kelley gave this Pommard 94 pt score. Which is about
the highest score I’ve seen in my career for Pommard. The most recent 4 years the scores have been as low as 93+, to 94 each year - except the
2020 is 94+. That is quite consistent - particularly for a Burgundy.
I was more aware that the Arnoux is just an infant (and an argument could be strongly made of me having committed Infanticide - I could feel that - quite clearly)
Often in my years of drinking I have tasted the ‘vintage’, the particular character of a vintage apart from the vineyard and climate and Vigneron.
The 2019 Red Burgundy - at this stage, is a very specific vintage, of bright-ripe fruit. (note to self - I need to taste the fine Bordeaux that has arrived and observe
that correlation - do I taste the vintage in the very same way. Oh my - what about Napa? I’ve not opened any of the very fine Napa wines and identified the vintage in the same manner)
For sure I must grab the Pommard Les Regiens 2019 that are in our warehouse for my personal pleasure.
We have 1 other flavor of Pommard from this producer I must check it out.
Now - I do remember (I think it is the 2019) opening some Volnay and Santenay and being absolutely in a field of delight. For Burgundy lovers like myself - OMG - I’ve really rambled on
I am so sorry…
I’m embarrassed, for Burgundy lovers I imagine you will relate - How many (few) moments there have been

I have to see what I can go back and acquire… IMHO- if you have a beloved Burgundy, 2019 is a year to indulge… - the Pillot - which has never stood out from the crowd for me,
and is about 1/10th the cost of the Arnoux, however, I’m thinking it may be the vintage.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
I just picked up some '19 Trapet Chapelle and Latricières.

So far I have consumed 5 bottles of '19 red Burgundy including 3 Mongeard’s - GE, Bourgogne and Bourgogne Hautes-Côtes de Nuits Les Dames, Lumpp Givry and Hudelot Clos Vougeot. 2 wines that cost around $300 and 3 sub $60 wines and I really liked them all. This vintage has great acidity, lively red fruits and nice concentration, everything is in balance. It is really in my wheelhouse and I plan on picking up quite a few more wines, although mostly in the under $200 range. I think Greg provided a great list of wines to target.
